question: You have A♠A♣ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: AA raises from every position. 3 BB is the standard sizing for every hand. Hardin's five reasons to raise: show strength, gain initiative, thin the field, build the pot, steal the blinds. Uniform sizing means opponents cannot read your hand strength.

why_wrong.1: Limping AA signals weakness with a capped range. You surrender fold equity and give everyone a cheap flop.
why_wrong.2: Never fold AA preflop.
why_wrong.3: Raising larger with strong hands telegraphs strength. Always 3 BB regardless of hand strength.

why_wrong.1: Calling AKo OOP in a 3-bet pot is 2.4x less profitable than 4-betting.
why_wrong.2: AKo is ahead of most 3-bet ranges except AA/KK. Fold is wrong.
why_wrong.3: Shoving folds out hands you want calls from.

question: You have 9♥9♦ in MP. You raised 3 BB, CO 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: 99 from MP vs CO 3-bet = fold. CO's 3-bet range vs MP includes JJ+, AK at minimum. 99 is behind JJ+ and flipping with AK. OOP at 100 BB, fold. Set-mining implied odds in 3-bet pots do not work at 100 BB effective stacks.

why_wrong.1: Calling 99 OOP in a 3-bet pot — you need to flop a set to win and implied odds don't cover it at 100 BB.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting 99 into JJ+/AK — almost always behind or flipping.
why_wrong.3: Shoving 99 is a massive overplay.

explanation: AA 5-bet jams or raises facing a 4-bet. AA dominates even the tightest 4-bet ranges. At 100 BB effective stacks, getting all-in preflop with AA is always correct.

why_wrong.1: Calling AA vs a 4-bet is second-best — get it all in with the best hand.
why_wrong.2: Never fold AA facing a 4-bet.
why_wrong.3: Checking is not an option when facing a raise.

question: You have Q♠Q♦ in BB. You 3-bet to 10.5 BB, CO 4-bets to ~22 BB. What do you do?
explanation: QQ vs a 4-bet from CO at 100 BB: call is the standard line. QQ is behind KK+ and flipping with AKs in CO's 4-bet range. At 100 BB calling (not 5-bet jamming) keeps the pot manageable — if CO 5-bets jam, you face a tough fold.

why_wrong.1: Folding QQ vs a 4-bet at 100 BB is too tight — call and evaluate postflop.
why_wrong.2: 5-bet jamming QQ into KK+/AKs puts you in a terrible spot if called.
why_wrong.3: Checking is not an option when facing a raise.

question: You have J♦J♣ in BB. You 3-bet to 10.5 BB, UTG 4-bets to ~22 BB. Action on you. What do you do?
explanation: JJ vs UTG 4-bet from BB = fold. UTG's 4-bet range is QQ+, AKs at minimum. JJ is behind QQ+ and flipping with AKs. Hardin: if your opponent is 4-betting a strong value range, fold JJ-QQ at 100 BB. The raise/fold line.

why_wrong.1: Calling JJ OOP in a 4-bet pot vs UTG's tight range leads to expensive spots on every overcard board.
why_wrong.2: 5-bet jamming JJ into QQ+/AKs — almost always behind or flipping.
why_wrong.3: Checking is not an option when facing a raise.
